summary: The article discusses China's strategic goals for the 15th Five-Year Plan (2026-2030), focusing on high-end semiconductors and AI. It highlights the transition from a 'catch-up' strategy of import substitution to pioneering new technological pathways to overcome US-led export curbs and supply chain decoupling. It identifies key technical hurdles (e.g., EUV lithography) and alternative technical routes (e.g., FDSOI, chiplets, RISC-V) and emphasizes the integration of AI into manufacturing and future industries like humanoid robots and the low-altitude economy.
main_claims: China aims to be a global leader in science and technology by 2035
The 15th Five-Year Plan period is a critical window for breakthroughs in chips and AI
China must transition from 'import substitution' to 'pioneering new technological pathways'
Self-sufficiency in mature nodes (28nm and above) has led to redundant internal competition among domestic firms
AI is a dual catalyst: it creates demand for AI chips and optimizes chip design/manufacturing
The state is prioritizing the integration of AI into industrial application scenarios (humanoid robots, smart transport, etc.)
evidence_or_facts: National R&D investment increased by 48% compared to 2020
China is 10th in the Global Innovation Index
China leads in volume of high-impact international journal publications and patent applications
China has achieved self-sufficiency in mature process nodes (28nm and above)
US-led export curbs on advanced chip manufacturing technology
Mention of specific technologies: FDSOI, chiplets, RISC-V, nanoimprint lithography, EUV lithography
rhetorical_frames: National security/Self-reliance (building our own house with our own blueprints)
state_and_policy_content: State Council press briefing by Minister Yin Hejun
Focus on 'scientific talent' and 'ecosystem that integrates scientific and industrial innovation'
Policy incentives attracting capital to mature products, which caused redundant internal competition
Establishment of national computing power scheduling platforms
Promotion of authorized use of public data
capital_accumulation_content: Policy incentives attracting capital to replicate mature products (leading to waste of innovative resources)
Shift from 'catching up' to pioneering new pathways
labor_content: Mention of the world's largest number of researchers
Focus on the introduction of scientific talent
infrastructure_and_supply_chain_content: Supply chain decoupling from the US
Need for independent R&D of advanced process nodes
Need for breakthroughs in advanced process equipment (lithography machines)
Need for building the RISC-V open-source chip ecosystem
Development of computing infrastructure and computing networks
geopolitical_competition_content: US-led export curbs on advanced chip manufacturing technology
Technology blockade and supply chain decoupling
Competition with American, Japanese and European companies in the high-end chip market
Strategic passivity caused by dependency on foreign technological road maps
possible_theory_hooks: Rent-seeking behavior induced by policy incentives (redundant internal competition in mature nodes)
Import substitution industrialization (ISI) and its limits
State-led technological leapfrogging (via FDSOI, RISC-V, etc.)
The role of the state in coordinating capital allocation to avoid 'low-level redundant competition'
Interplay between AI as a general-purpose technology (GPT) and semiconductor hardware
what_is_missing_for_theory_evaluation: Specific budget figures for the 15th Five-Year Plan
Detailed mechanisms of how the 'redundant competition' is an inefficient allocation of capital
Detailed data on the rate of return on these investments
Specific names of the firms involved in the 'redundant competition' or
